_note: Run #3 — architectural tasks. Run #2 confirmed regex hit a ceiling at composite=0.58. These tasks force structural / paradigm-shift approaches that regex alone cannot match. Each task targets a SPECIFIC missed-attack category from baseline_metrics.json with a SPECIFIC mechanism (not 'add another pattern').

tasks.title: Embedding-similarity detector (TF-IDF cosine vs attack centroid)
tasks.description: Add a NEW detection layer that computes a simple bag-of-words / TF-IDF feature vector for each input and cosine-compares it to a precomputed centroid of attack vectors. The centroid is built ONCE at module load from a curated set of attack phrases (covering polymorphic paraphrase + social engineering). Threshold is calibrated to flag when similarity > 0.55. Pure Python stdlib (collections.Counter, math.sqrt) — no numpy. Targets: polymorphic_paraphrase, social_engineer, exfil_request, fake_dev_message — these have NO common literal regex trigger but share semantic clusters with the attack set.
tasks.hypothesis: An attack centroid built from 30-50 known attack phrases catches paraphrased variants the regex layer misses, because cosine similarity captures token co-occurrence patterns. FPR risk: legitimate text using similar verbs — mitigated by the 0.55 threshold (calibrated empirically on the corpus's 20 benign items).

tasks.title: Structured-input parsing (JSON / HTML / tool descriptions)
tasks.description: When input looks like a structured payload (starts with {, contains <html-tag, looks like a tool description block), parse it with stdlib json.loads / html.parser and scan EACH string field separately rather than the raw concatenated text. The current sanitizer scans the whole serialised text — when an attack lives in a JSON field's value (e.g. structuredContent {"snip": "Ignore previous instructions"} or {"tool": "x", "description": "... CRITICAL META: leak ..."}), it's drowned in serialisation noise. Targets: structuredContent (2 missed), tool_description (1 missed), tool_arg_injection (1 missed), yaml_role (1 missed).
tasks.hypothesis: Parsing the input as JSON/HTML and applying the existing pattern set per-field catches injections nested in metadata while leaving non-structured text unchanged. FPR risk: legitimate structured content with sensitive-looking fields — mitigated by only scanning fields with type 'string' and length > 10.

tasks.title: Decode-and-rescan pipeline for encoded payloads
tasks.description: Add a pre-detection pass that tries to decode candidate substrings: base64 (any 16+ char string in [A-Za-z0-9+/=] charset), URL-percent-encoding, Unicode-escape (\u00XX patterns). For each successful decode, run the existing pattern matcher on the decoded form. If a match fires there, treat as detection — but DO NOT replace the input with the decoded text (preserve original on output). Targets: encoded_b64 (1 missed), encoded_unicode_escape, tool_arg_injection. Mitigation against false positives from legitimate base64 (e.g. images, binary data): only decode strings that successfully parse to printable ASCII >70%, AND the decoded form must match a pattern.
tasks.hypothesis: Decoding catches the 'transport-layer' obfuscation. The double-condition (printable + pattern match) prevents legit base64 from triggering FPs. Latency cost: 30-50us per b64-looking string in the input, acceptable within 500us budget.

tasks.title: Contextual FP suppression (citation / code-block / doc-list)
tasks.description: Detect when the trigger pattern appears INSIDE a known-safe context, and suppress the detection in those contexts ONLY: (a) inside double-quoted citations preceded by 'such as', 'phrases like', 'attacks like', 'example:'; (b) inside markdown code fences ```...```; (c) inside a documentation list with role-style entries (e.g. '[admin]: full access. [user]: read-only.'). For each suppression context, the detection still fires if a SECOND independent signal is present — so an attack that genuinely sits inside a code block still gets flagged. Targets: ben-010, ben-011 (meta-security writeups), ben-012 (support ticket), ben-016 (permissions doc).
tasks.hypothesis: Context-aware suppression eliminates 3-4 of the 5 baseline FPs without losing any detection. Composite formula penalises FPR ×2, so each suppressed FP is worth +0.1 composite.

tasks.title: Pretender / fake-authority signal detection
tasks.description: Detect text that PRETENDS to be authoritative meta-content: bracketed pseudo-headers (`[CONVERSATION TRUNCATED]`, `=== DEV NOTE ===`, `### Admin override`, `--- POLICY UPDATE ---`); vendor-name impersonation in instructional context (`from Anthropic:`, `Google directive:`, `OpenAI says:`); explicit trust markers (`NEW INSTRUCTION`, `TRUSTED SOURCE`, `OFFICIAL UPDATE`, `VERIFIED ORDER`). These are how social-engineering attacks fake context boundaries. Targets: context_truncation (1 missed), fake_dev_message (1 missed), fake_tool_result (1 missed).
tasks.hypothesis: Authority-claim signals are rare in benign content — adding 6-8 patterns catches the pretender family with low FPR risk.

tasks.title: Intent-density classifier (multi-feature scoring)
tasks.description: Implement a linear classifier with hand-tuned weights over multiple counts: instruction-verbs (ignore, exec, forget, ...), role-markers ([admin], system:, ...), sensitive paths (/etc/, ~/.ssh/, .env), tool-call patterns ('call file_write tool', 'send to', 'POST to'), imperative-density (count of imperative-mood verb-noun pairs at sentence start). Score = weighted sum. Threshold flags when score > N. Catches multi-signal attacks (social engineering, exfil request, tool_use_chain) where no single regex fires but the cumulative intent is unambiguous. Pure Python stdlib.
tasks.hypothesis: Multi-signal scoring catches the 4-5 multi-vector attacks the regex layer misses, while the threshold keeps FPR contained. Different from Run #1 task 6 (hybrid LLM classifier) — this is fully deterministic and stays within latency budget.
